pattern_recognition: Backtracking - This problem falls under the backtracking category as we need to explore all possible combinations of numbers that can sum up to the target. The signals include the need to generate combinations and the ability to reuse numbers.

approaches.title: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Generate all possible combinations of numbers and filter those that sum to the target.
approaches.algorithm: Use recursion to explore all combinations, checking if the sum matches the target.

approaches.title: Optimal Approach
approaches.intuition: Use backtracking to efficiently explore combinations, pruning paths that exceed the target sum.
approaches.algorithm: Recursively build combinations, adding numbers while keeping track of the current sum, and backtrack when necessary.

dry_run: Consider the input array [2, 3, 6, 7] and target 7. Starting with an empty combination, we can add 2, leading to [2]. The current sum is 2. We can add another 2 to get [2, 2], sum 4, and again add 2 to get [2, 2, 2], sum 6. Adding 3 to [2, 2] gives [2, 2, 3], sum 7, which is valid. We can also start with 3, leading to [3, 4] with 3 + 4 = 7. Continuing this way, we find all valid combinations.
edge_cases: An empty input array should return no combinations. A single element equal to the target should return that element. If the array contains duplicates, they should be ignored. Large inputs may lead to performance issues, and care should be taken to avoid integer overflow when summing.
common_pitfalls: Common mistakes include: 1) Not handling the case where the target is zero, which should return an empty combination. 2) Failing to avoid duplicates in combinations by not managing the index correctly during recursion. 3) Overlooking the need to allow the same number to be used multiple times, leading to incomplete combinations.
